title: Apply: The Road to the Civil War
description: Apply your understanding of the events leading up to the Civil War with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction
The Road to the Civil War
Apply: The Road to the Civil War

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Charles Sumner’s Annual Report of the American Anti-Slavery Society (1855).**

Believing in the sure prevalence of Almighty Truth, I confidently look forward to the good time when both will unite, according to the sentiments of the fathers and the true spirit of the Constitution, in declaring Freedom, and not Slavery, *national,* while Slavery, and not Freedom, shall be *[[☃ definition 1]].* Then will be achieved that Union, contemplated at the beginning, against which the storms of faction, and the assaults of foreign power shall beat in vain, as upon the Rock of Ages; and LIBERTY, *seeking a firm foothold, will have at last* WHEREON TO STAND AND MOVE THE WORLD.

**Which event is Sumner referring to that would declare “Freedom, and not Slavery, national, while Slavery, and not Freedom, shall be sectional”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The end of the Mexican-American War
The passage of the Kansas-Nebraska Act
The election of Abraham Lincoln
The secession of the South

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1860 editorial in the *Providence Post.***

The [Republicans] would stand as a wall of fire against the admission of any more slave states. . . . They would change the Supreme Court. They would, in short, pursue such a course as would instantly unite the South against the general government and make a separation of the states the only remedy.

**Which statement best expresses the main idea of the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Republicans do not intend to end slavery and will continue to compromise with the South to maintain the Union.
Republicans want to completely change the government of the United States and will do so even if it means seceding.
Republicans want to go back to the policy of not allowing slavery to extend to states north of the 36°30′ line.
Republicans aim to stop the increase in the number of slaveholding states, and this will lead to the southern states to leave the Union.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1860 pamphlet titled “The Slavery Question.” This pamphlet was directed at free voters in Ohio, particularly those of the northern Democratic party.**

Freemen of Ohio! Will you dishonor the dead—will you [[☃ definition 1]] the living—will you deprive [[☃ definition 2]] of its inheritance, by [[☃ definition 3]] the fraud thus practiced on the conservative, constitutional, anti-slavery sentiment, by the profession of which the Democratic party achieved success in the [[☃ definition 7]] contest! Friends of [[☃ definition 6]]! Will you endorse a doctrine which mocks your opinions, which disregards your wishes, which tramples on your rights as members of the Democratic party? 

If the Supreme Court is right, your doctrine is a delusion. Will you agree that it is so, because [[☃ definition 8]] judges, in a pretended decision, not binding on your or on the country, pretend that the doctrines of [[☃ definition 4]] are the true doctrines of those who framed the Constitution? Friends of the [[☃ definition 5]]! Are you men, and will you sink in your devotion to party the honest convictions of your own minds and the cherished wishes of your own free hearts? Voters of a State dedicated to freedom! Shall slavery subdue you now and forever?

**Which event was the author of this pamphlet reacting to?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: John C. Calhoun, who promoted the idea that the Constitution required the federal government to uphold slavery
a failed 1846 proposal to ban slavery in territory that the U.S. won in the Mexican-American war
the idea that people who lived in a territory should be able to determine whether that territory would allow slavery

options.explanation: “The slavery question.” African American Pamphlet Collection. From the [Library of Congress.](https://www.loc.gov/resource/rbaapc.26400/?sp=3&st=image)

choices.content: The Supreme Court’s decision in *Dred Scott v. Sandford*
The decision to go to war with Mexico
The outbreak of mob violence in Kansas
The secession of the southern states from the Union

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1860 pamphlet titled “The Slavery Question.” This pamphlet was directed at free voters in Ohio, particularly those of the northern Democratic party.**

Freemen of Ohio! Will you dishonor the dead—will you [[☃ definition 1]] the living—will you deprive [[☃ definition 2]] of its inheritance, by [[☃ definition 3]] the fraud thus practiced on the conservative, constitutional, anti-slavery sentiment, by the profession of which the Democratic party achieved success in the [[☃ definition 7]] contest! Friends of [[☃ definition 6]]! Will you endorse a doctrine which mocks your opinions, which disregards your wishes, which tramples on your rights as members of the Democratic party? 

If the Supreme Court is right, your doctrine is a delusion. Will you agree that it is so, because [[☃ definition 8]] judges, in a pretended decision, not binding on your or on the country, pretend that the doctrines of [[☃ definition 4]] are the true doctrines of those who framed the Constitution? Friends of the [[☃ definition 5]]! Are you men, and will you sink in your devotion to party the honest convictions of your own minds and the cherished wishes of your own free hearts? Voters of a State dedicated to freedom! Shall slavery subdue you now and forever?

**What argument is the author making in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: John C. Calhoun, who promoted the idea that the Constitution required the federal government to uphold slavery
a failed 1846 proposal to ban slavery in territory that the U.S. won in the Mexican-American war
the idea that people who lived in a territory should be able to determine whether that territory would allow slavery

options.explanation: “The slavery question.” African American Pamphlet Collection. From the [Library of Congress.](https://www.loc.gov/resource/rbaapc.26400/?sp=3&st=image)

choices.content: The Supreme Court has too much power and must be checked.
The extension of slavery to new territories will continue to happen despite protests.
The Supreme Court’s recent decision in *Dred Scott* has violated the rights of free men.
Anti-slavery sentiment is too strong, and the country must divide.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Jefferson Davis’s 1858 speech to the Mississippi Senate.**

I say to you here as I have said to the Democracy of New York, if it should ever come to pass that the Constitution shall be [[☃ definition 1]] to the destruction of our rights so that we shall have the mere right as a feeble minority unprotected by the barrier of the Constitution to given an [[☃ definition 2]] negative vote in the Halls of Congress, we shall then bear to the federal government the relation our colonial fathers did to the British crown, and if we are worthy of our lineage we will in that event [[☃ definition 3]] our rights even if it be through the process of revolution. . . .

Now, as in 1851, I hold separation from the Union by the State of Mississippi to be the last remedy—the final alternative.

**Davis justifies the secession of the South by comparing secession to which event in U.S. history?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: used in an improper or corrupt way

options.explanation: Jefferson Davis, Speech to the Mississippi Senate, [Digital Public Library of America,](https://dp.la/primary-source-sets/secession-of-the-southern-states/sources/771) 1858.

choices.content: The drafting of the Constitution
The admission of New York as a state
The Revolutionary War
The Boston Tea Party

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best describes the message of the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: [“Forcing Slavery Down the Throat of a Freesoiler”](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Forcing_Slavery_Freesoilers_Throats.jpg) by John L. Magee is in the public domain.

options.alt: The cartoon shows a large White man who is tied down. Two older White man stand on a wooden platform and hold him down by his hair. The platform is labeled "Democratic platform" and some of the wooden planks are labeled Kansas, Cuba, and Central America. Another two White men stand on the large man's chest. They are pushing a Black man into his mouth. The Black man is saying, "MURDER!!! Help—neighbors, help. O my poor wife and children." The caption under the cartoon reads, "Forcing Slavery Down the Throat of a Freesoiler."
options.caption: “Forcing Slavery Down the Throat of a Freesoiler” by John L. Magee, 1856

choices.content: The South’s economy relies on enslaved labor, and any attempts to ban slavery will result in the collapse of the South.
Recent legislation promotes the expansion of slavery and forces those opposed to slavery to accept its continued practice.
The Constitution affirms that the institution of slavery is constitutional, so political parties must protect the institution.
Abolitionists and formerly enslaved people must appeal to the political parties to secure the end of slavery.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**This image was created in response to which event?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: [“Forcing Slavery Down the Throat of a Freesoiler”](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Forcing_Slavery_Freesoilers_Throats.jpg) by John L. Magee is in the public domain.

options.alt: The cartoon shows a large White man who is tied down. Two older White man stand on a wooden platform and hold him down by his hair. The platform is labeled "Democratic platform" and some of the wooden planks are labeled Kansas, Cuba, and Central America. Another two White men stand on the large man's chest. They are pushing a Black man into his mouth. The Black man is saying, "MURDER!!! Help—neighbors, help. O my poor wife and children." The caption under the cartoon reads, "Forcing Slavery Down the Throat of a Freesoiler."
options.caption: “Forcing Slavery Down the Throat of a Freesoiler” by John L. Magee, 1856

choices.content: The enactment of the Kansas-Nebraska Act
The start of the Mexican-American War
The election of Abraham Lincoln
The secession of the Southern states

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1857 article about *Dred Scott v. Sandford* (1857) in the *Albany Evening Journal*.**

Many things in the monstrous decision of the U.S. Supreme Court shock the moral sense of the public. But the [[☃ definition 1]] of the blow which [[☃ definition 2]] the citizenship of all the Free colored people in the United States, has fallen with a stunning force on all who have been taught that justice is [[☃ definition 3]] on man, and that Christianity is the social law of Humanity. The half million of men and women paralyzed by the [[☃ definition 4]] logic of the decision of the case of Dred Scott, which disfranchises them on the soil on which they were born, will be to all free and uncorrupted souls a complete denial of the bad law and worse conscience, with which the Supreme Court has pronounced its departure from Republicanism and its entrance into Slavery.

**According to the excerpt, what is one effect of the Supreme Court’s *Dred Scott* decision?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: godless; not in keeping with religious principles

choices.content: Birthright citizenship was guaranteed for all Americans.
More people turned from Christianity to atheism.
The Republican Party lacked support and dissolved.
Free Black men could not be considered citizens.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Dred Scott Case,” an 1857 article in the *Richmond Enquirer*. **

A prize, for which the athletes of the nation have often wrestled in the halls of Congress, has been awarded at last, by the proper umpire, to those who have justly won it. The *nation* has achieved a triumph, *[[☃ definition 1]]* has been [[☃ definition 2]], and abolitionism has been staggered and stunned. Another supporting pillar has been added to our institutions; the assailants of the South and enemies of the Union have been driven from their [[☃ definition 3]]; a patriotic principle has been pronounced; a great, national, conservative, union saving sentiment has been proclaimed. An [[☃ definition 4]] of the constitutionality of the Missouri Compromise . . . points of [[☃ definition 5]] [[☃ definition 6]] between the friends and enemies of the institutions of the South; all of which, it will be seen, have been [[☃ definition 7]] established in accordance with the sense of the Southern people.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument about the *Dred Scott* decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the practice of favoring one part of a country over others
a strategic point where troops assemble prior to battle
unable to be brought into harmony or agreement
in a way that leaves no doubt

choices.content: It legitimized the abolitionist movement and guaranteed the end of slavery.
It gave southern states the right to secede from the United States.
It was decided fairly and will help to end division in the United States.
It has further divided the nation on the issue of slavery.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Dred Scott Case,” an 1857 article in the *Richmond Enquirer*. **

A prize, for which the athletes of the nation have often wrestled in the halls of Congress, has been awarded at last, by the proper umpire, to those who have justly won it. The *nation* has achieved a triumph, *[[☃ definition 1]]* has been [[☃ definition 2]], and abolitionism has been staggered and stunned. Another supporting pillar has been added to our institutions; the assailants of the South and enemies of the Union have been driven from their [[☃ definition 3]]; a patriotic principle has been pronounced; a great, national, conservative, union saving sentiment has been proclaimed. An [[☃ definition 4]] of the constitutionality of the Missouri Compromise . . . points of [[☃ definition 5]] [[☃ definition 6]] between the friends and enemies of the institutions of the South; all of which, it will be seen, have been [[☃ definition 7]] established in accordance with the sense of the Southern people.

**Which group would most likely support the ideas in the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the practice of favoring one part of a country over others
a strategic point where troops assemble prior to battle
unable to be brought into harmony or agreement
in a way that leaves no doubt

choices.content: Southern Democrats
Northern Republicans
Freesoilers
Abolitionists

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Abraham Lincoln’s 1857 speech at the Illinois Republican State Convention.**

We are now far into the fifth year, since a policy was initiated, with the [[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]], and confident promise, of putting an end to slavery agitation. Under the operation of that policy, that agitation has not only, not ceased, but has constantly [[☃ definition 3]]. In my opinion, it will not cease, until a crisis shall have been reached, and passed—"A house divided against itself cannot stand." I believe that this government cannot long endure, permanently, half slave and half free. I do not expect this Union to be dissolved; I do not expect the house to fall; but I do expect it will cease to be divided. It will become all one thing, or all the other.

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Slavery needs to be legalized and protected by the Constitution to fully solve the issue of sectionalism.
The United States must remain unified, as other countries have sought to invade and take possession of U.S. territories.
The tension caused by sectionalism has been resolved, now that the division of free and slave states is firmly decided.
Sectionalism will remain an issue until the United States comes to a unified decision about slavery.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1854 article in the *New-Haven Daily Register.***

. . . The Administration was about to abandon the principles of the Compromise of 1850, and [[☃ definition 6]] itself by an adoration of [[☃ definition 7]]! and it has given two or three war dances and whoops to get up an early panic among the early friends of that measure, without success. It now shows the cloven foot, in its attacks on Mr. Douglas' [[☃ definition 1]], which, in organizing a territorial government for Nebraska, leaves the question of slavery to be settled by the people, whenever Nebraska shall become a State — according to the spirit, if not the letter of the very compromise measure of 1850, which it was so fearful the Administration would not adhere to! 

— This proves what we have always asserted, that the [[☃ definition 2]] admiration . . .  for the Compromise measures, was a [[☃ definition 3]]; for were it not so, we would not now find it objecting to the application of the principle to Nebraska. The Compromise of 1850 is well understood to be a "finality" — [[☃ definition 4]] all previous action, and designed to stop all [[☃ definition 5]] of the slavery question, in or out of Congress. Well, Nebraska comes up for a territorial government, and Mr. Douglas very wisely, justly, and in accordance with the principles of the Compromise, and to prevent agitation, proposes to leave that question to the people of that territory to settle by themselves. 

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: public debate and protest to achieve change
belief that slavery should not be extended into new U.S. territories

choices.content: The Compromise of 1850 was successful because Nebraska accomplished its goal of becoming a state.
The Compromise of 1850 failed, as people continue to fight over the expansion of slavery to Nebraska.
The Compromise of 1850 succeeded, as can be seen in Nebraska where the people are peacefully deciding on the issue of slavery.
The Compromise of 1850 was struck down by the Supreme Court after Nebraska sued for their statehood.
